title: practical english usage - Part 336
content:
Mean and meaning are 'false friends’  for speakers of some European languages.

2 mean       and   think,      meaning          and   opinion are you using the word?) Note also the preposition in What do you mean by 'hermetic'? (= In what sense not Excuse me. What does 'hermetic' mean? (     What-means 'hermetic'?) Note the structure of questions with mean.

Perhaps is often pronounced ‘ praps’  by British people.

Julius Caesar is perhaps the greatest of Shakespeare's early plays.

Maybe/Perhaps it'll stop raining soon.

go in mid-position (►  200).

perhaps is used more often than maybe in a formal style, and in this case it may These two words mean the same. They  are both common. In British English, 525  maybe        and   perhaps For no matter what, etc, ►    253.

Is there anything the matter?

There's something the matter with the TV.

There is often used as a 'preparatory subject’  (►  20).

What's the matter with Frank today?

Nothing's the matter with the car - you're just a bad driver.

Something's the matter with my foot.  Is anything the matter?

It means 'wrong (with)’ .

We   use the matter (with) after something, anything, nothing and what.

524 the    matter        (with) I've been married to you for 25  years and I still don't understand you.

She got married to her childhood sweetheart.

We   can also use get/be married to with an object.

3 get/be      married         to Word    Problems      from    A to  Z  Section 31 vocabulary •  527  means not We must help her by all possible means. (     We must help her by all means.) By all means get a new coat, but don't spend more than £150.

'Can I borrow your sweater?' 'By all means.' or 'it is all right to .. .’  Compare: permission or to encourage somebody to do something, and means 'of course’ By all means is not the same as by all possible means. It is used to give 2 by   all/any/no         means For other words with singular forms ending in -s, ►     11 7.3.

There are several means of transport on the island.

the railway. (NOT   . . . a new mean of communication. ..) In the 19th century a new means of communication was developed - Both the singular and the plural of means end in -s.

1  singular      and   plural     ending      in  -s 527 means I've been meaning to phone you for weeks.

But perfect progressive forms can be used to refer to intentions.

What does that strange smile mean? (     What is that strange smile meaning?) Mean is not normally used in progressive forms when it refers to meanings.

6  no   progressive         form What do you mean by waking me up at this time of night?

What do you mean, I can't sing?

What do you mean . . .? can express anger or protest.

5  What      do   you     mean       .. .2 She lives in Southport -I mean Southampton.

I mean is also used to introduce corrections.

Let's go and see Jake on Saturday. I mean, we could make an early start...

real meaning.

In informal speech, I mean often acts as a connector or ‘ filler’ , with little (BUT NOT I mean that it's not right                                           . .) A hundred pounds for a thirty-hour week. I mean, it's not right, is it?

not followed by that.

When  I mean introduces a comment it can be close to I think or I feel, but it is Would you like to come out tonight? I mean, only if you want to, of course.

It was a terrible evening. I mean, they all sat round and talked politics.

He's funny - I mean, he's really strange.

additional details.

/ mean is used informally as a 'discourse marker’  to introduce explanations or 4 I mean If you decide to try the exam, it will mean studying hard.

The Fantasians have invaded Utopia. This means war!

Mean (= involve, have as a result) can be followed by a noun or an -ing form.

vocabulary •         miss You've just missed her - she went home five minutes ago.

If you don't hurry we'll miss the train, (     .. .lose the train.) The station's about five minutes' walk, straight ahead. You can't miss it.

How could he miss an easy goal like that?

late for somebody/something.

Miss often expresses the idea of failing to contact somebody/something, or being 1 'fail   to  contact',     'be  late   for' 529 miss not ( ...) I don't mind what the children do after they leave home.
